reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Formal Languages

map.condition: Checking if CFLs are closed under union / concatenation / Kleene star
Checking if CFLs are closed under intersection or complementation
map.use: \text{CFLs are NOT closed under } \cap \text{ or complementation}

map.condition: Intersection of a CFL with a regular language
Proving a language is not context-free
map.use: \text{Pumping Lemma for CFLs: } \forall w \in L,\ |w| \geq p,\ w = uvxyz,\ |vy| \geq 1,\ |vxy| \leq p,\ uv^ixy^iz \in L

map.condition: Checking if a grammar generates a language like a^n b^n

formulas.name: Context-Free Language (CFL) Definition
formulas.latex: L \text{ is a CFL} \iff \exists \text{ CFG } G = (V, \Sigma, R, S) \text{ such that } L = L(G)
formulas.plain: L is a CFL if and only if there exists a Context-Free Grammar G = (V, Sigma, R, S) such that L = L(G)
formulas.whenToUse: When verifying whether a language qualifies as context-free by exhibiting a generating CFG.

terms.means: set of production rules of the form A -> alpha

formulas.trap: A language is CFL if ANY CFG exists for it — the grammar need not be unique or in any normal form.

formulas.name: CFL = PDA Equivalence
formulas.latex: L \text{ is CFL} \iff L \text{ is accepted by a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)}
formulas.plain: L is CFL if and only if L is accepted by a Pushdown Automaton (PDA)
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks about the machine model equivalent to CFLs.

terms.means: Pushdown Automaton — finite automaton with an unbounded stack

formulas.trap: Deterministic PDA (DPDA) recognises only DCFLs (proper subset of CFLs); non-deterministic PDA is needed for all CFLs.

formulas.name: Chomsky Hierarchy Containment
formulas.plain: Regular SUBSET DCFL SUBSET CFL SUBSET Context-Sensitive SUBSET Recursively Enumerable
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing language classes or determining which machine/grammar type is needed.
formulas.trap: Every regular language is a CFL but not vice versa; a^n b^n is CFL but not regular.

formulas.name: Closure under Union
formulas.plain: If L1 and L2 are CFLs then L1 union L2 is also a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether the union of two CFLs is always a CFL.
formulas.trap: This is proved by adding a new start symbol S -> S1 | S2 in the combined grammar.

formulas.name: Closure under Concatenation
formulas.plain: If L1 and L2 are CFLs then L1 concatenated with L2 is also a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether concatenation of two CFLs is context-free.

terms.means: language of all strings w1w2 where w1 in L1 and w2 in L2

formulas.trap: Proved by grammar rule S -> S1 S2 using separate non-terminals for each grammar.

formulas.name: Closure under Kleene Star
formulas.plain: If L is a CFL then L* is also a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether the Kleene closure of a CFL remains context-free.

terms.means: zero or more concatenations of strings from L

formulas.trap: L+ (one or more) is also closed; L* includes the empty string epsilon even if L does not.

formulas.name: NOT Closed under Intersection
formulas.plain: The intersection of two CFLs is NOT necessarily a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether intersection of two CFLs is always a CFL (the answer is NO).

terms.means: set of strings belonging to both L1 and L2

formulas.trap: Classic counterexample: L1 = {a^n b^n c^m} and L2 = {a^m b^n c^n} are both CFL, but L1 cap L2 = {a^n b^n c^n} is NOT CFL.

formulas.name: NOT Closed under Complementation
formulas.plain: The complement of a CFL is NOT necessarily a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether complementation preserves context-freeness.

terms.means: complement of L with respect to Sigma*

formulas.trap: This follows from De Morgan's law: if CFLs were closed under complement, they would be closed under intersection (since L1 cap L2 = complement(complement(L1) union complement(L2))), which is a contradiction.

formulas.name: Intersection with Regular Language
formulas.plain: CFL intersected with a Regular language is always a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When filtering a CFL by a regular constraint or deciding if the result is CFL.
formulas.trap: This is proved by running a PDA and DFA in parallel (product construction); the result is a PDA, hence CFL.

formulas.name: Closure under Reversal
formulas.plain: If L is a CFL then the reversal of L (all strings reversed) is also a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about reversal closure of CFLs.

terms.means: language of reversals of all strings in L

formulas.trap: Reversal is closed for CFLs; proved by reversing all RHS of grammar productions.

formulas.name: Pumping Lemma for CFLs
formulas.latex: \forall L \in \text{CFL},\ \exists p > 0 \text{ s.t. } \forall w \in L,\ |w| \geq p:\newline w = uvxyz,\ |vy| \geq 1,\ |vxy| \leq p,\ \forall i \geq 0:\ uv^ixy^iz \in L
formulas.plain: For every CFL L, there exists pumping length p > 0 such that for all w in L with |w| >= p, w can be split as w = uvxyz where |vy| >= 1, |vxy| <= p, and for all i >= 0, uv^i x y^i z is in L
formulas.whenToUse: When proving a language is NOT context-free by deriving a contradiction.

terms.means: at least one of v, y is non-empty
the pumpable middle section has bounded length

formulas.trap: Unlike pumping lemma for regular languages (two parts vxy), CFLs split into FIVE parts (uvxyz) — v and y are pumped simultaneously. Pumping lemma can only DISPROVE CFL membership, not prove it.

formulas.name: Classic Non-CFL: a^n b^n c^n
formulas.plain: L = {a^n b^n c^n | n >= 1} is NOT a context-free language
formulas.whenToUse: As a benchmark: any language requiring counting three equal-length segments is not CFL.

terms.means: equal counts of three distinct symbols

formulas.trap: a^n b^n IS CFL (two equal segments); a^n b^n c^n is NOT CFL (three equal segments).

formulas.name: Grammar for a^n b^n (n >= 1)
formulas.plain: S -> aSb | ab  generates {a^n b^n | n >= 1}
formulas.whenToUse: When writing a CFG for strings with equal numbers of a's followed by b's (n >= 1).
formulas.trap: Using S -> aSb | epsilon generates n >= 0 (includes empty string). If n >= 1 is required, use S -> ab as base case, not epsilon.

formulas.name: Grammar for a^n b^n (n >= 0)
formulas.plain: S -> aSb | epsilon  generates {a^n b^n | n >= 0}
formulas.whenToUse: When writing a CFG for equal a's and b's including the empty string.
formulas.trap: This grammar also generates the empty string (n=0). Distinguish from n >= 1 variant carefully in GATE questions.

formulas.name: Grammar for Even Palindromes
formulas.plain: S -> aSa | bSb | epsilon  generates even-length palindromes over {a, b}
formulas.whenToUse: When asked for a CFG producing palindromes over a two-symbol alphabet.

terms.means: string that reads the same forward and backward

formulas.trap: For odd-length palindromes include S -> a | b as additional base cases.

formulas.name: Grammar for Balanced Parentheses / Dyck Language
formulas.plain: S -> SS | (S) | epsilon  generates balanced parentheses (Dyck language)
formulas.whenToUse: When constructing a grammar for properly matched brackets/parentheses.
formulas.trap: The Dyck language is CFL (generated by CFG); nesting of any depth is handled by the recursive rule (S).

formulas.name: Grammar for Strings with Unequal Counts: |a| != |b|
formulas.plain: The language of strings over {a,b} where count of a's is not equal to count of b's is a CFL
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether comparing (unequal) counts of two symbols yields a CFL.
formulas.trap: L = {w | #a = #b} is CFL; L = {w | #a != #b} is also CFL (complement of a DCFL). But {w | #a = #b = #c} is NOT CFL.

formulas.name: Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)
formulas.latex: \text{Every production is: } A \rightarrow BC \text{ or } A \rightarrow a
formulas.plain: In CNF every production is either A -> BC (two non-terminals) or A -> a (single terminal)
formulas.whenToUse: When converting a CFG to CNF for CYK parsing or theoretical proofs.

terms.means: non-terminal symbols (with B, C != S if epsilon in L)

formulas.trap: Every CFL has a CNF grammar; conversion may increase the number of productions but does not change the language.

formulas.name: Greibach Normal Form (GNF)
formulas.latex: \text{Every production is: } A \rightarrow a\alpha,\ a \in \Sigma,\ \alpha \in V^*
formulas.plain: In GNF every production starts with exactly one terminal followed by zero or more non-terminals
formulas.whenToUse: When constructing a PDA with exactly one stack push per input symbol, or eliminating left recursion.
formulas.trap: GNF eliminates left recursion; every CFL (without epsilon) can be converted to GNF.

formulas.name: Membership Problem is Decidable (CYK)
formulas.latex: \text{Given CFG } G \text{ and string } w:\ w \in L(G)? \text{ is decidable (CYK in } O(n^3)\text{)}
formulas.plain: Given a CFG G and a string w, deciding whether w is in L(G) is decidable using the CYK algorithm in O(n^3)
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about parsing complexity or decidability of membership for CFLs.
formulas.trap: Membership is decidable for CFLs but the universality problem (L(G) = Sigma*?) is UNDECIDABLE for CFLs.

formulas.name: Undecidable Problems for CFLs
formulas.latex: \text{Undecidable for CFGs: } L(G_1) \cap L(G_2) = \emptyset?,\ L(G) = \Sigma^*?,\ L(G_1) = L(G_2)?
formulas.plain: Undecidable for CFGs: intersection emptiness, universality (L(G)=Sigma*?), and equivalence (L(G1)=L(G2)?) are all undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which decision problems about CFLs/CFGs are undecidable.

terms.means: universality: does the grammar generate all strings?

formulas.trap: Emptiness (L(G) = empty?) IS decidable for CFLs; universality is NOT. Do not confuse them.

formulas.name: Emptiness and Finiteness are Decidable
formulas.latex: L(G) = \emptyset? \text{ and } |L(G)| < \infty? \text{ are both decidable for CFGs}
formulas.plain: Both emptiness (L(G)=empty?) and finiteness (L(G) finite?) are decidable for context-free grammars
formulas.whenToUse: When asked which decision problems are decidable for CFLs.

terms.means: no string is generated by the grammar

formulas.trap: Decidability contrast: empty/finite = decidable; universal/equivalent/ambiguous = undecidable.
